title: Banks’ negative gearing reaction
full_text:
BUDGET 2026 Investors have punished Australia’s banks in the wake of the Albanese government’s move to end new negative gearing arrangements and reduce capital gains tax relief.
It was a double whammy for the biggest investor lender, Commonwealth Bank, whose reckoning was combined with a weak third-quarter profit update. CBA shares tumbled 10.4 per cent to $153.67, Westpac shed 2.8 per cent to $35.57, ANZ lost 1.6 per cent to $34.57 and National Australia Bank closed 1.5 per cent lower at $36.86.
A predicted pullback in investor lending, which will flow through to bank earnings, provided an early glimpse at the scope of Jim Chalmers’ tax grab. From July 1, 2027, negative gearing arrangements will only apply to new builds, for example.
Citi banks analyst Tom Strong said the budget amounted to “downside risk to mortgage credit”. He said changes to capital gains tax and negative gearing were likely to diminish investor demand for existing homes, which may restrict the supply of new houses, at odds with the policy’s ambitions.
He also said the change could drive people to put more money into their own homes. “From a tax perspective, incremental investment into primary residences looks more attractive given tax-free capital gains and the loss of negative gearing for investment properties if that were the alternative investment decision,” Mr Strong said.
Macquarie analysis found that borrowing power for investors would tumble 10-20 per cent under the government’s plans. “The key impact of these policies is likely to be a moderation in credit growth,” Macquarie’s Victor German said.
“And with bank share prices correlated with house prices/house price expectations, any slowdown in the housing market could see banks underperform.” Atlas Funds Management chief investment officer Hugh Dive said he expected a chilling effect on credit growth. But he wondered if the wash-up may drive investors into bank stocks as they chased franking credits and yield.
“High fully franked dividend stocks are more attractive post the changes,” he said, notwithstanding some banks were already trading at excessive valuations.
“Looking forward to credit growth, the investor loans are looking to fall quite dramatically,” the fund manager said.
The government hopes to collect $10.5bn from the banking sector by the end of the decade via the major bank levy, introduced in 2017, which imposes a 0.06 per cent annual charge on major bank liabilities. The budget reveals the government now expects to raise an additional $380m by the end of the decade.
The government has also reduced reporting requirements under the Financial Accountability Regime. Smaller banks will no longer need to report low value breaches. CBA’s profit was down 1 per cent at $2.7bn. It also warned arrears and bad loans were tracking higher, in a return to levels not seen since the start of the pandemic. CBA loaded up $200m in provisions reflecting a more pessimistic economic outlook.
summary: Australia's banks faced significant share price declines following the government's decision to end new negative gearing arrangements and reduce capital gains tax relief, with Commonwealth Bank shares dropping 10.4% amid a weak profit report.
author: David Ross
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Commonwealth Bank shares fell 10.4% to $153.67, while other major banks also experienced declines.
From July 1, 2027, negative gearing will only apply to new builds, potentially reducing investor demand for existing homes.
The government expects to raise an additional $380 million from the banking sector by the end of the decade through the major bank levy.
